planting.note: Direct sow in fall (September–October in zone 9) or very early spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; cumin is an annual that matures its seed crop in warm, dry weather (not humid) — zone 9 spring growing conditions are ideal, with the crop seeded and harvested before the humid summer arrives.

directions: Direct sow seeds thinly in well-drained, lean, sandy to loamy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun in fall or early spring; thin to 6 inches. Cuminum cyminum (cumin) is the annual herb whose seeds are the third most-consumed spice in the world after salt and black pepper — the warm, earthy, aromatic, slightly bitter cumin flavor is foundational to the spice profiles of Indian, Mexican, Middle Eastern, North African, and Turkish cuisines, appearing in curry powders, garam masala, chili powder, taco seasoning, hummus, and countless spice blends. The small (6–18 inch), slender, feathery-leaved annual herb native to Egypt and the eastern Mediterranean is rarely grown in North American home gardens, yet it is straightforward to grow in well-drained, dry, infertile soil in full sun in climates with warm, dry summers or cool, dry springs. In zone 9, fall planting produces spring harvest before summer humidity arrives. Harvest when seed heads turn brown and dry; cut stalks and hang to dry in paper bags, then thresh out seeds. Growing your own cumin produces seeds with fresher, more intense aroma than store-bought.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Herb Growing
Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Cuminum cyminum

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, loamy to sandy soil in full sun; ajwain is a cool-season annual native to the Near East and South Asia that performs best in warm-dry spring conditions — in zone 9, fall to winter planting produces the best spring seed crop.

directions: Direct sow seeds in fall or early spring in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; thin to 8–12 inches. Trachyspermum ammi (ajwain or carom seed) is an annual herb in the carrot family (Apiaceae) native to Egypt and the Near East, cultivated extensively in India, Pakistan, and Iran for its small, ribbed seeds with an intensely pungent thyme-like but more piercing aroma and flavor — the dominant compound is thymol, which is also found in thyme, but present in much higher concentrations in ajwain seeds. In Indian cooking, ajwain is used in breads (naan, paratha), lentil dishes (dal), fritters (pakoras), pickles, and dry spice blends where its strong, distinctive flavor contributes essential character that cannot be substituted. The seeds are used whole (dry-roasted to release their oil before adding) or ground; they also have significant traditional medicinal use in South Asian medicine for digestive complaints. The plant resembles a small, upright dill or caraway with feathery, blue-green leaves and compound white flower umbels in spring; harvest when seed heads dry and turn brown.
sources: Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Trachyspermum ammi
University of California Cooperative Extension — Specialty Herbs

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (August–September in zone 9) for overwintering as a small rosette and bolting and seeding the following spring; caraway is a biennial (or winter annual in mild climates) that requires cool temperatures to flower and set seed — in zone 9, it can be grown as a cool-season annual by fall-sowing for early spring seed harvest.

directions: Direct sow seeds in fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; caraway does not transplant well — sow in place. Carum carvi (caraway) is a biennial or short-lived annual herb producing the anise-flavored, ribbed, crescent-shaped seeds indispensable in German and Central European rye bread, sauerkraut, cheese (Havarti caraway), and liqueurs (aquavit, Kümmel); the seeds are also a traditional flavoring for sausages, pickles, and roasted vegetables across northern and eastern European cuisines. The fresh leaves and roots are also edible (roots somewhat parsnip-like) and used in Scandinavian and German cooking. In the first year, caraway forms a low rosette of finely divided, feathery, carrot-like leaves; in the second year (or the same spring after fall sowing in mild climates), it sends up 1.5-to-2-foot stems topped with compound white flower umbels, setting seed in May–June. Harvest seed heads when they turn brown and before they shatter; thresh out seeds and dry fully before storing. In zone 9, treat as a fall-sown winter annual for best results.
sources: University of Wisconsin Cooperative Extension — Herb Growing

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; calamint is native to dry rocky hillsides around the Mediterranean and demands excellent drainage — it will not survive in wet, heavy soil, especially during winter.

directions: Plant in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; excellent drainage is essential — calamint will not tolerate wet soil, especially in winter. Calamintha nepeta (calamint or lesser calamint) is a delightfully fragrant perennial herb native to dry, rocky Mediterranean hillsides, producing a compact mound of small, soft, gray-green, somewhat aromatic leaves smelling of a blend of mint, oregano, and thyme, and masses of tiny, tubular, white to pale-lavender-pink flowers in loose, branching spikes from July through September. The tiny flowers are exceptional for small native bees, mining bees, and bumblebees, which visit the plant in large numbers when in bloom — calamint is now recognized as one of the best small perennial herbs for native bee support, producing enormous quantities of nectar-rich flowers. In Italian cuisine, calamint ('nepitella') is a traditional flavoring for sautéed mushrooms and legume dishes in Tuscan and Roman cooking — a flavor somewhere between mint and oregano that is difficult to substitute. Shear plants to 4–6 inches after the main bloom flush to encourage compact regrowth and often a second flowering.

common_name: Korean Mint (Korean Licorice Mint, Blue Licorice)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost or direct sow in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Korean mint self-seeds prolifically in suitable gardens and can naturalize readily.

directions: Start seeds indoors or direct sow in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Korean mint tolerates more shade than most agastaches and performs well in partial shade with at least 4 hours of sun. Agastache rugosa (Korean mint) is an East Asian perennial herb producing upright, branching stems to 3–4 feet tall with large, broadly ovate, intensely aromatic leaves smelling distinctly of anise-licorice-mint and dense, showy, 4-to-6-inch spikes of small, blue-purple to lavender-purple flowers from July through September that are outstandingly attractive to bumble bees, honeybees, and both hummingbird and hawk moths. In Korean and Chinese traditional medicine, Korean mint (huo xiang or Korean perilla) is a widely used digestive herb with a long history of use for gastrointestinal complaints and as an aromatic flavoring. In the garden it is primarily grown as an ornamental pollinator plant — one of the best agastaches for zones with both heat and cold, performing well from zone 5 through zone 9 if given well-drained soil. The flowers dry well and the foliage remains strongly aromatic when dried. Self-seeds freely.
